cover.html: Welcome everyone. This session is about one of your biggest expansion opportunities — metrics. Not a nice-to-have, not a future roadmap item. Right now, today, in the accounts you're already in. Let's get into it.
hook.html: I want to start with a simple question. Are you ready to win? Because the metrics market is one of the clearest greenfield opportunities Elastic has right now. Your customers are already spending on this — they're just spending it somewhere else. That changes today.
market.html: Let's look at where the money is. Datadog alone is doing over $2 billion in metrics revenue. Splunk, Dynatrace, Grafana — that's another $1.6 billion on top. We're talking about a $5 billion addressable market that your customers are actively spending in, right now. The question isn't whether they're buying. It's whether they're buying from you.
storage.html: This is worth pausing on. Last year we stored metrics at 25 bytes per datapoint. Today it's 3.75 bytes. That's a 6x improvement in storage efficiency — and it's already in production. When a customer asks why Elastic is cheaper, this is a big part of the answer. We fundamentally re-engineered how we store time series data.
competitive.html: When you're in a competitive deal, these are your numbers. 30 times faster than Prometheus. 30 times faster than Mimir. 8 times faster than ClickHouse. Storage efficiency that beats everyone. And PromQL compatibility that matches Mimir. Elastic isn't asking customers to compromise — we're asking them to upgrade.
cheaper.html: And by the way — we're also 4 times cheaper than Datadog. I want that to land. Not 10% cheaper. Not 20% cheaper. Four times cheaper. When your champion needs to make the business case internally, this is the slide they screenshot and send to their CFO.
hook-migration.html: The product is there. The pricing is there. The question now is: how do you actually land this? How do you get a customer who's been on Prometheus or Datadog for years to make the move? The answer is: you make it embarrassingly easy.
prometheus.html: Most of your Kubernetes customers already live in Prometheus. They're not going to rip and replace their instrumentation — and they don't have to. We speak Prometheus natively. Same ingest, same PromQL queries, 2.5 times better storage efficiency, and you can be live in one day. Meet them where they are.
promql.html: 80% of enterprise SREs already know PromQL. That's their language. Now Kibana speaks it too. You're not asking them to learn something new — you're giving them a better engine under the same interface. That's the framing: everything you know, everything you've built, but faster and cheaper.
migrate.html: The migration story is three steps. Connect your Prometheus source. We automatically convert your dashboards and alerts. Go live. One day. We've seen this work in production. The 'migration risk' objection — the one that kills deals — is neutralized. You're not asking them to bet their monitoring on an untested migration. You're showing them the path.
kubernetes.html: 93% of companies are running or evaluating Kubernetes. That's your universe. And out of the box, Elastic gives them cluster health, node metrics, CPU and memory tracking, root cause analysis — all pre-built, all in Kibana. No instrumentation work. No dashboard building. It's just there. That's what 'lovable OOTB experience' actually means.
mcp-demo.html: This is where it starts to feel like the future. Elastic's MCP server connects directly to Claude Desktop. Your SRE can ask 'what's wrong with my cluster' in plain English and get back real data — latency, error rates, anomaly explanations, blast radius. No dashboard hunting. No query writing. Just a conversation.
plain-english.html: The MCP integration means your systems become conversational. The skills load automatically based on what data is available. Kibana surfaces the right visualization for the context. What you're seeing here isn't a demo — it's a capability that's shipping. Your customers can talk to their infrastructure like they'd talk to a colleague.
world.html:
Every IT exec in your pipeline right now is having the same conversation with their board. Ship faster. Use AI. Cut time to market. And it's working — developers are shipping in days what used to take months. AI-generated  
code is hitting production constantly. That's the good news.

The bad news is nobody thought about what happens on the other side.
slack-convo.html: Here's what the other side looks like at about 3pm...
ai-scale.html:
That's funny. Until it's your team. And here's why it keeps happening.
AI didn't just make your developers faster. It made your production environment faster — more deployments, more services, more agents running in prod. But your SRE team is the same size it was two years ago.                                                               
The ratio broke. Dev velocity went up 10x. The surface area to observe went up 10x. The number of humans watching it? Zero x.
That's not a cost problem. That's an asymmetry problem. And it only gets worse.
ns-transition.html: So here's the real question. It's 3am. Something just broke. An AI agent pushed a change. A dependency failed. A cascade started. Who's watching? Your SRE is asleep. Your on-call rotation is stretched. Your runbooks are out of date. What happens next?
ns-intro.html: Nightshift. This is Elastic's answer to that question. Not a dashboard. Not an alert. An AI SRE that works the shift nobody wants to work — the one that starts when your team goes home and ends when they come back.
nightshift-hero.html: Meet your AI SRE. Always watching, always learning, always ready. What you're seeing on the right is a real incident resolution. Memory exhaustion detected, Redis pool exhaustion identified as root cause, fix applied — pool limit increased to 250. Four minutes and twenty-three seconds. Zero human pages. That's not a future state. That's the product.
nightshift-architecture.html: Let's look under the hood. At the top is the SRE Agent — that's what takes action. Below that is the Context Layer — the intelligence layer that stores failure signatures, fix outcomes, and learned patterns. It feeds off your telemetry and artifacts. And it connects to the tools your team already uses: GitHub, Slack, Jira, PagerDuty. Nightshift doesn't replace your stack. It sits on top of it.
nightshift-capabilities.html: Two things to highlight here. First — distribution. Nightshift meets your team wherever they work. Kibana, Slack, CLI, IDE, CI/CD pipelines — it's the same intelligence everywhere. Second — action connectors. When Nightshift decides to act, it can reach into Ansible, Terraform, ArgoCD, ServiceNow. Investigation and remediation, end to end.
nightshift-ai-index.html: The Context Layer is what makes Nightshift smarter over time. It's not just querying your telemetry — it's building a living model of how your systems behave. Failure signatures, knowledge indicators, pattern library. And it works across any observability backend — Elasticsearch, S3, Databricks, even Datadog or Grafana. It sits above your stack, not inside it.
nightshift-brain.html: This is one of the most important design decisions in Nightshift. Every customer gets their own brain — private, trained on their incidents, their topology, their runbooks. But optionally, with a single opt-in, you can contribute anonymized failure patterns to a shared brain. No names, no values, no topology — just the pattern. Redis pool exhaustion looks the same whether it's at a fintech or a retailer. Everyone gets smarter together.
nightshift-ai-economics.html: This is why Nightshift is economically viable when other AI SRE approaches aren't. You cannot send petabytes of raw telemetry to an LLM. So we don't. We compress it through three layers — raw telemetry to knowledge indicators to significant events. By the time it reaches the model, it's kilobytes, not gigabytes. Token efficiency is a core differentiator. It's how we keep the cost of AI operations manageable at scale.
nightshift-live-demo.html: Let me show you this live. What you're about to see is Nightshift detecting a real incident, reasoning through the blast radius, identifying the root cause, and taking action — without a human in the loop. Watch the timeline.
nightshift-reveal.html: Wake up to this. Not a page. Not an alert. A resolution. The incident happened at 3:43am. Nightshift detected it, diagnosed it, fixed it, and confirmed all services healthy — by 3:47am. Four minutes and twenty-three seconds. Two actions taken. Zero human pages. Your on-call engineer woke up and it was already over. That's what you're selling.
cta_slide.html: Your move. Three clear paths depending on who you're talking to. Existing Elastic customers — navigate to the metrics buyer, the story is already there. Prometheus and Grafana shops — one backend, 30 times faster, overnight migration. Price-sensitive Datadog accounts — 50% cheaper, and you can be live tomorrow. Pick the motion that fits the account and start the conversation.
sko-sessions.html: Before you leave — here are the sessions you need to get to this week. The Metrics and Vision session Tuesday at 1:30, the Agentic Observability deep-dive Wednesday. If you're newer to the space, the Observability 101 is worth your time. And for field engineering — there are dedicated hands-on sessions that will get you demo-ready. Put these in your calendar now.
thank-you.html: Thank you. Questions are welcome — find me here or at elastic.co/observability for everything we covered today. Let's go win some metrics deals.
